title: Computing the Arc-Deletion Distance to Orchard Networks is NP-hard
author_string: Peng Li, Zhiwei Liu, Yangjing Long
abstract: Phylogenetic networks generalize phylogenetic trees by allowing reticulate evolutionary events such as horizontal gene transfer and hybridization. Among the many subclasses of phylogenetic networks, orchard networks have attracted increasing attention due to their structural and algorithmic properties. In this paper, we study the arc-deletion distance to orchard networks, defined as the minimum number of reticulate arcs whose deletion transforms a phylogenetic network into an orchard network. We prove that computing this distance is NP-hard via a polynomial-time reduction from the Degree-3 Vertex Cover problem. Our result establishes the computational intractability of this proximity measure and contributes to the complexity theory of phylogenetic network transformations.
